name: comparative-matrix
description: Generate structured comparisons and decision matrices across analyzed frameworks. Use when (1) comparing multiple frameworks or approaches side-by-side, (2) making architectural decisions between alternatives, (3) creating best-of-breed selection documentation, (4) synthesizing findings from multiple analysis skills into actionable decisions, or (5) producing recommendation reports for technical stakeholders.
capabilities: Collects and normalizes analysis outputs from multiple frameworks into comparable formats
Generates structured comparison matrices across standardized dimensions
Applies decision heuristics based on scalability, developer experience, or production requirements
Documents recommendations with clear rationale and trade-off explanations
Produces structured output artifacts including summary tables and detailed analysis
author: Dowwie
securitySummary: This is a prompt-only documentation skill containing only markdown templates, decision heuristics in pseudo-code, and comparison frameworks. All 22 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The scanner misidentified Pydantic (a validation library) as a cryptographic algorithm, markdown code blocks showing template syntax as shell backtick execution, and skill integration documentation as system reconnaissance. No executable code, network calls, filesystem access, or command execution capabilities exist.
valueStatement: Technical teams need structured frameworks to compare multiple tools objectively when making architectural decisions. This skill synthesizes analysis outputs into decision matrices with clear recommendations and documented trade-offs for any framework comparison.
limitations: Does not execute code or perform live framework testing
Requires input analysis from other skills to function effectively
Does not provide runtime performance benchmarks
Cannot access external documentation or APIs directly

useCases.title: Evaluate architectural options
useCases.description: Compare multiple agent frameworks when designing new systems with specific requirements for scalability and maintainability.

useCases.title: Select best frameworks
useCases.description: Create evidence-based recommendations for tool selection based on team skills, project constraints, and long-term maintainability.

useCases.title: Document technical decisions
useCases.description: Produce clear comparison documentation for stakeholders showing trade-offs and rationale behind framework selections.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: Use comparative-matrix to compare [Framework A] and [Framework B] across these dimensions: typing approach, async model, state management, configuration style, and extensibility pattern.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Framework Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate a best-of-breed matrix comparing [Framework A], [Framework B], and [Framework C] for [use case]. Focus on typing, async, state, config, and extensibility.

promptTemplates.title: Production Readiness
promptTemplates.prompt: Apply production-first heuristics to evaluate [framework] for mission-critical deployment. Document observability needs, error boundaries, and typing requirements.

promptTemplates.title: Team Fit Assessment
promptTemplates.prompt: Use DX-first heuristics to compare frameworks for a small team doing rapid iteration. Consider learning curve, configuration style, and code complexity.

bestPractices: Provide detailed analysis outputs from other skills as input for richer comparisons
Specify your decision criteria (scalability, DX, or production priorities) upfront
Review the trade-off log to ensure all compromises are understood before deciding
antiPatterns: Using this skill without prior framework analysis as input
Skipping the trade-off documentation when making final decisions
Ignoring the decision heuristics that match your project requirements

faq.question: What inputs does this skill need?
faq.answer: Analysis outputs from other skills like architecture-synthesis work best as input data for comparisons.

faq.question: What frameworks can it compare?
faq.answer: Any software frameworks. The skill uses flexible dimensions like typing, async, state, config, and extensibility.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. This is a prompt-based skill that synthesizes text inputs without making external network calls.

faq.question: Is my data safe with this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill only processes text locally within your AI conversation and does not store or transmit data externally.

faq.question: How many frameworks can it compare at once?
faq.answer: The skill handles any number of frameworks. More inputs create larger matrices but maintain the same comparison structure.

faq.question: How is this different from architecture-synthesis?
faq.answer: Comparative-matrix creates side-by-side framework comparisons. Architecture-synthesis designs new systems based on requirements.
